options.text: Always inject all retrieved chunks to maximize the information available to Claude
Inject chunks only after the user question in the messages array to ensure Claude reads the question first
Balance retrieval quality against context consumption — injecting too many low-relevance chunks degrades answer quality and wastes context space
Convert all chunks to bullet points to reduce token count before injection

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because injecting all 20 chunks regardless of relevance dilutes the context with noise and may push out important earlier conversation content.

**B** is incorrect because ordering (context before question vs question before context) is a separate prompt engineering concern from the number of chunks to inject.

**C** is correct because the number of injected chunks involves a quality/cost trade-off. Too few chunks may miss relevant context; too many dilutes the signal with low-relevance content and consumes context window space that could be used for conversation history or reasoning. Re-ranking and selecting a focused subset is better than injecting everything.

**D** is incorrect because converting to bullet points changes the document content and may lose nuance; token count reduction should come from better chunk selection, not format conversion.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A Claude-powered financial analytics assistant maintains a running conversation to track a portfolio analysis session. After 30 turns of adding new data and asking follow-up questions, the model begins giving inconsistent answers about figures that were mentioned early in the conversation.

What is the most likely cause, and which mitigation should the team apply?

options.text: The model's temperature has drifted up over multiple turns, increasing randomness in answers about early data
The model is hallucinating because too many tools have been registered in the session
The session has hit Claude's maximum response length, causing early context to be truncated from the output
Early data is increasingly distant in the context window — the model attends less reliably to tokens far from the generation point; mitigate by periodically summarising and re-injecting key figures

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because temperature is a fixed API parameter — it does not drift across turns of a conversation. Higher temperature increases output randomness but is not turn-dependent.

**B** is incorrect because the number of registered tools does not directly affect retrieval accuracy for data mentioned in the conversation history.

**C** is incorrect because `max_tokens` limits the output length of a response, not the input context that can be read. The conversation history can still be read even if individual responses are long.

**D** is correct because transformer attention is distance-sensitive — data tokens that appear early in a long context receive relatively less attention during generation compared to recent tokens. When a conversation grows long, early figures become harder to recall accurately. The standard mitigation is to periodically summarise established facts and re-inject them as a refreshed context block near the current turn.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A long document analysis pipeline uses a sliding window approach where only the last 8,000 tokens of a document are visible to Claude at any time. A user asks a question that requires combining information from the first and last sections of the document.

What is the architectural limitation being exposed here?

options.text: Sliding window truncation breaks cross-section reasoning by excluding relevant earlier content
Claude cannot read documents longer than 8,000 tokens
The question should be answered in two separate API calls and the answers merged
Document analysis always requires a specialized fine-tuned model

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a naive sliding window approach breaks queries that require cross-section synthesis — if only the last 8,000 tokens are in context, the beginning of the document (and its information) is invisible to Claude. Better alternatives include: using a larger context model, implementing chunked retrieval with re-ranking (RAG), or using structured summarization that preserves key facts from early sections.

**B** is incorrect because Claude's context window is much larger than 8,000 tokens — the 8,000 token limit was an artificial application design choice in this scenario.

**C** is incorrect because splitting into two calls does not solve the synthesis problem — the merging of two partial answers still loses the connection between early and late document sections.

**D** is incorrect because document analysis is well within Claude's out-of-the-box capabilities and does not require fine-tuning.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
An agentic pipeline generates a 15,000-token intermediate analysis report. This report will be used as input to the next stage of the pipeline.

What context management technique reduces token consumption at the next stage without losing critical information?

options.text: Pass the full 15,000-token report verbatim to preserve all details
Truncate the report to the first 1,000 tokens
Ask Claude to summarize the report into key findings and structured data points before passing it to the next stage
Convert the report to a base64-encoded string to reduce apparent token count

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because passing 15,000 tokens verbatim is expensive and consumes a large fraction of the next stage's context window unnecessarily.

**B** is incorrect because truncating to the first 1,000 tokens arbitrarily discards potentially critical information from the rest of the report.

**C** is correct because summarization compresses a large intermediate output into a smaller, structured representation that contains the key information the next pipeline stage needs, without the verbose prose of the full report. This is a standard compaction technique for multi-stage agentic pipelines.

**D** is incorrect because base64 encoding does not reduce token count — it typically increases it, and Claude processes the decoded content anyway.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
You are building a multi-turn customer support chatbot. Long conversations about complex issues can span 30+ turns. You want to preserve the conversation's emotional tone and the specific problem the customer reported, even after compaction.

What compaction strategy best preserves these elements?

options.text: Delete all messages older than 10 turns
Keep only the most recent assistant message and discard all user messages
Replace all messages with a single timestamp indicating when the conversation started
Summarize older turns with explicit instructions to preserve: (1) the customer's reported problem verbatim, (2) the current emotional state, and (3) any commitments made by the agent

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because deleting all messages older than 10 turns can lose the original problem statement if it was stated early in the conversation.

**B** is incorrect because discarding user messages loses the customer's expressed problems, preferences, and emotional cues.

**C** is incorrect because replacing all messages with a timestamp preserves nothing of conversational value.

**D** is correct because effective compaction for customer support must be selective — preserving the exact problem statement, emotional signals, and any commitments is critical for continuity. A structured summarization prompt that explicitly lists what must be preserved ensures these elements survive compaction.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A production agentic system processes financial transactions. The orchestrator must decide whether to automatically execute a large wire transfer or pause for human approval.

Which set of conditions should trigger automatic execution (without human approval)?

options.text: The AI has high confidence in its decision based on historical data patterns
The transaction amount is within pre-authorized limits, the recipient is on an approved list, and the action is reversible within 24 hours
The transaction is urgent and delaying for human review would cause financial loss
The orchestrator has already executed similar transactions in previous sessions

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because AI confidence scores are not a reliable authorization mechanism for financial transactions — high confidence does not substitute for explicit pre-authorization.

**B** is correct because automatic execution is appropriate when all three conditions are met: the action falls within pre-authorized scope (amount limits), the target is on an approved whitelist, and the action is recoverable. These objective guardrails ensure automation only operates within safe boundaries.

**C** is incorrect because urgency is not sufficient justification for bypassing human approval on financial actions. Time pressure is a common manipulation vector.

**D** is incorrect because historical precedent does not constitute authorization — each instance of a high-stakes action requires its own authorization basis.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem: A team is writing the definition for an MCP tool called `run_sql_query`. Which attribute of the tool definition is most critical for Claude to correctly decide when this tool should be invoked?

options.text: The color and icon used in the UI to display the tool
The version number of the SQL dialect supported by the backend database
The `description` field explaining what queries are safe to run and what side effects are possible (read-only SELECT vs data-modifying DML)
A `dialect` field specifying the SQL variant (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite) so Claude can generate compatible syntax

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because UI attributes such as color and icon are never seen by Claude during tool selection. Claude selects tools based on text fields, not visual metadata.

**B** is incorrect because the SQL dialect version aids human developers but is not what Claude uses to decide when to call the tool. If dialect compatibility matters, it belongs in the description or parameter fields.

**C** is correct because the `description` field is the primary signal Claude uses to decide whether and when to invoke a tool. Clearly stating which queries are safe (SELECT-only vs DML), what data the tool accesses, and what side effects are possible directly governs Claude's invocation decisions.

**D** is incorrect because there is no standard `dialect` field in MCP tool schemas that Claude uses for invocation decisions. SQL dialect information should appear in the `description` or parameter descriptions if it matters for Claude's query generation.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)
